source: pandapower / MATPOWER cases PEGASE, RTE, Polish, GB, Illinois, IEEE-300 (BSD); raw grids not redistributed
meanfield_control_note: The degree-matched configuration-model control is unreliable for these grids because of their heavy degree-1/degree-2 leaf tail, so the theoretical mean-field value 2.5 is used as the reference rather than the per-grid control.
interpretation: GENERALIZATION (robust): the CEBH transfer failure holds across a population of ten real transmission grids (200-9241 buses; PEGASE, French RTE, Polish, GB, US). The degree-moment formula under-predicts the observed bond-percolation threshold in every grid (10/10 all-positive gap, mean 0.30, larger than the 0.22 road gap), and the zero-parameter junction-composition anchor beats CEBH in 100% of grids, cutting the mean absolute error about fourfold even though power grids are non-planar. BOUNDARY (the informative half): the finite-size exponent read-out does not carry over as a two-dimensional signal. The cluster-size-distribution exponent of the power grids is tau_F = 2.42 +- 0.23 (range 2.00-2.69), near the mean-field value 2.5 and well above the near-planar road value 2.12 (close to 2D 2.055). Junction composition sets the threshold value in both domains, but the near-two-dimensional exponent signal is specific to near-planar road geometry in this test. This separates the two parts of the result: the threshold mechanism transfers, whereas the finite-size critical-class evidence is geometry-specific.
